title: C# Advanced — Paper 8
description: Advanced C# covering Async/Await & Task Basics, Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Generic Variance, Dynamic Types, Advanced Pattern Matching, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, Advanced Exception Handling, and Memory Management.

questions.text: What is the difference between a sealed class and a sealed record in C# 9+?

options.text: A sealed record cannot have properties; a sealed class can.
Both prevent inheritance; for records, sealing also prevents derivation of other records from it and means the compiler-generated equality and ToString are final.
A sealed record is always a value type.
Sealing a record disables the 'with' expression.

questions.text: What is a deadlock in async code and what common mistake causes it?

options.text: Deadlocks in async code are caused by using too many tasks.

A common deadlock is calling .Result or .Wait() on a Task inside a synchronization-context-aware environment (e.g., ASP.NET classic or WPF): the continuation needs the context to resume, but the context is blocked waiting for the task.
Deadlocks only occur in multi-threaded code with locks, not async code.
Using ConfigureAwait(false) always causes deadlocks.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the Partitioner class in the Task Parallel Library?

options.text: Partitioner manages thread priority in parallel operations.
Partitioner divides a data source into chunks for parallel processing; custom partitioners allow fine-grained control over how data is distributed across threads in Parallel.For/ForEach and PLINQ.
Partitioner is used to partition namespaces in assemblies.
Partitioner provides a thread-safe queue for task distribution.

questions.text: What does the [EnumeratorCancellation] attribute on a CancellationToken parameter in an async iterator enable?

options.text: It makes the iterator automatically retry on cancellation.

It allows the CancellationToken passed to GetAsyncEnumerator() (via WithCancellation()) to flow into the iterator method, so the iterator method's token parameter receives the consumer's cancellation token.
It converts the async iterator into a synchronous one.
It registers the iterator for automatic disposal.

questions.text: How does a generic type parameter with multiple constraints work? E.g., 'where T : class, IComparable<T>, new()'

options.text: Only one constraint can be active at a time; the others are ignored.
All constraints must be satisfied simultaneously: T must be a reference type, implement IComparable<T>, and have a public parameterless constructor.
Multiple constraints create an OR condition; T must satisfy any one.
The new() constraint is ignored when other constraints are present.

questions.text: What is generic type variance and why can't it be applied to classes?

options.text: Variance applies to classes; interfaces and delegates do not support it.

Variance (covariant 'out' and contravariant 'in') can only be applied to generic type parameters of interfaces and delegates; classes and structs are invariant because they can be both read from and written to, which would break type safety.
Variance is not supported in C# at all.
Classes support covariance but not contravariance.

questions.text: What is the purpose of using ExpandoObject over a Dictionary<string,object>?

options.text: ExpandoObject is faster than Dictionary<string,object>.

ExpandoObject supports dynamic member access syntax (expando.Name) via the DLR, can have event handlers added dynamically, and integrates with the 'dynamic' keyword; Dictionary requires string-key indexer syntax.
ExpandoObject provides type safety; Dictionary does not.
ExpandoObject serializes to JSON automatically.

questions.text: What is a 'var pattern' in C# pattern matching?

options.text: A var pattern matches only variables, not constants.
The 'var' pattern always matches any value (including null) and introduces a new variable of the inferred type: 'x is var y' always succeeds and assigns x to y.
A var pattern only works with value types.
A var pattern matches when the variable is uninitialized.

questions.text: In C# 10+, what is a 'global using' directive?

options.text: A using directive that applies only to a single file.
A 'global using' directive applies to all files in the project automatically, avoiding repetition of common using statements across files.
A global using makes types available across all assemblies in a solution.
A global using directive is only valid for static classes.

questions.text: What is a 'try/catch/when' exception filter's key safety benefit over rethrowing?

options.text: Exception filters produce shorter code but have identical behavior to catch+rethrow.

An exception filter evaluates the condition before stack unwinding; if the filter is false, the runtime looks for the next handler without unwinding the stack, preserving the full original call stack for better diagnostics.
Exception filters allow swallowing exceptions silently.
They are only available in .NET 5+ and not in .NET Framework.

questions.text: What is the large object heap (LOH) in .NET and when does an allocation go there?

options.text: All reference types are allocated on the LOH.
Objects larger than approximately 85,000 bytes are allocated on the large object heap, which is collected less frequently and is not compacted by default, potentially causing fragmentation.
The LOH is used for all gen 2 objects.
The LOH only holds string objects.

questions.text: What does Task.FromResult<T>(value) do?

options.text: It creates a new Task<T> that runs on the thread pool.
It creates an already-completed Task<T> with the specified result, useful for returning a synchronous result from an async interface without creating a real async operation.
It schedules value to be returned when the task is awaited later.
It converts a synchronous method into an async method.

questions.text: What is the DataflowBlock in System.Threading.Tasks.Dataflow and how does it relate to TPL?

options.text: DataflowBlock is the same as ConcurrentQueue<T>.

TPL Dataflow provides message-passing blocks (ActionBlock, TransformBlock, etc.) that can be linked into pipelines; each block processes messages concurrently with configurable parallelism, built on top of the TPL.
DataflowBlock replaces async/await for all pipeline processing.
DataflowBlock is only available in .NET Framework.

questions.text: What does IAsyncDisposable provide over IDisposable?

options.text: IAsyncDisposable disposes resources on a background thread automatically.
IAsyncDisposable provides DisposeAsync() which returns a ValueTask, enabling cleanup of resources that require async operations (e.g., flushing a buffer) without blocking.
IAsyncDisposable is for COM interop only.
IAsyncDisposable replaces IDisposable in all .NET 6+ types.

questions.text: What is a generic repository pattern and how does it use generic type constraints?

options.text: A generic repository uses Func<T> to define queries.

A generic repository IRepository<T> where T : class defines CRUD operations for any entity type T, using constraints to ensure T can be managed by the ORM, promoting reuse without duplicating code per entity.
Generic repositories require reflection to work.
A generic repository can only be used with database entities.

questions.text: What is the purpose of IReadOnlyList<T> vs IList<T> from a variance perspective?

options.text: They are identical; the 'ReadOnly' prefix is just a naming convention.
IReadOnlyList<T> is covariant (out T) because it only exposes reads; IList<T> is invariant because it supports both reads and writes, making covariance unsafe.
IReadOnlyList<T> is contravariant; IList<T> is covariant.
IReadOnlyList<T> does not support indexing; IList<T> does.

questions.text: How does 'dynamic' interact with operator overloading in C#?

options.text: dynamic ignores operator overloading and uses default arithmetic.
When operands are 'dynamic', the DLR resolves operator overloads at runtime using the actual runtime types, so custom operators are respected even though the static type is 'dynamic'.
Operator overloading is disabled for dynamic types.
Dynamic operands always use boxing and generic arithmetic.

questions.text: What is extended property patterns in C# 10+?

options.text: Extended property patterns allow pattern matching on extension methods.
Extended property patterns allow nested property access in patterns using dot notation: '{ Address.City: "Paris" }' instead of requiring nested patterns '{ Address: { City: "Paris" } }'.
Extended property patterns match any number of properties simultaneously.
They are an extended syntax for initializing property values.

questions.text: What is the 'init' accessor and how does it differ from 'set'?

options.text: init and set are interchangeable.
'init' allows a property to be set only during object initialization (constructor or object initializer) and not after; 'set' allows mutation at any time after construction.
'init' makes a property read-only from all contexts including the constructor.
'init' is only valid on record types.

questions.text: What does AggregateException.Handle() do?

options.text: Handle() re-throws all unhandled exceptions.

Handle() calls a predicate for each inner exception; if the predicate returns true, that exception is considered handled; if any inner exception returns false (unhandled), a new AggregateException containing only the unhandled ones is thrown.
Handle() logs all exceptions and suppresses them.
Handle() is equivalent to iterating InnerExceptions and calling catch.

questions.text: What is a pinned object in .NET GC and when is pinning necessary?

options.text: Pinned objects are permanently promoted to gen 2.

Pinning prevents the GC from moving an object during collection (compaction), which is necessary when passing a managed object's address to unmanaged code via P/Invoke or unsafe pointer operations, ensuring the pointer remains valid.
Pinning is required for all value types passed to native code.
The GC never moves objects, so pinning is never needed.

questions.text: How does Task.Run differ from Task.Factory.StartNew in the context of async lambdas?

options.text: Task.Factory.StartNew correctly handles async lambdas; Task.Run does not.
Task.Run correctly unwraps async lambdas (Func<Task>) returning a Task that represents the async operation; Task.Factory.StartNew returns Task<Task> and requires explicit Unwrap() for async lambdas.
They are identical for async lambdas.
Task.Run does not support async lambdas at all.

questions.text: What is the covariant return type feature in C# 9+ and how does it apply to class hierarchies?

options.text: Covariant return types allow overriding with a less derived return type.
C# 9+ allows overriding a virtual method with a more derived return type: a method returning Animal can be overridden to return Dog, as long as Dog derives from Animal.
Covariant return types apply only to generic methods.
Covariant return types are only available on interface methods.

questions.text: What is MemoryPool<T> in .NET and how does it relate to Memory<T>?

options.text: MemoryPool<T> is a static utility class for allocating arrays.

MemoryPool<T> provides a reusable pool of Memory<T> buffers via Rent(size), returning an IMemoryOwner<T> that wraps a Memory<T>; returning the owner releases the buffer back to the pool, reducing allocations.
MemoryPool<T> is equivalent to ArrayPool<T>.
MemoryPool<T> allocates on the large object heap.

questions.text: What does the 'checked' block do when applied to a floating-point operation?

options.text: It causes an OverflowException on floating-point overflow.
The 'checked' keyword has no effect on floating-point arithmetic; it only affects integer arithmetic. Floating-point overflow produces Infinity or NaN, not an exception.
It converts floating-point results to integers before checking.
It enables IEEE 754 strict mode for floating-point.

questions.text: What is an invariant generic type in C# and why is List<T> invariant?

options.text: List<T> is covariant because it can be read from.

An invariant type does not support variance: List<string> is not assignable to List<object> because List<T> supports both Add(T) (input) and T this[int] (output); allowing covariance would let you add objects to a List<string>, breaking type safety.
List<T> is contravariant because it only supports Add().
Invariant means the type parameter must be a value type.

questions.text: What is the tuple element name feature in C# 7+ and how does it improve code?

options.text: Tuple element names are stored at runtime and accessed via reflection.

Named tuple elements allow referring to components by name instead of Item1/Item2: '(string Name, int Age) person = ("Alice", 30); person.Name' is clearer than 'person.Item1'; names are a compile-time feature encoded in attributes.
Named elements in tuples create new named types like anonymous types.
Tuple element names are only available for System.Tuple, not value tuples.

questions.text: What is the purpose of GC.AddMemoryPressure() in .NET?

options.text: It manually triggers a garbage collection.

It informs the GC of significant unmanaged memory allocations so the GC can adjust its collection frequency, preventing situations where managed memory is low but unmanaged memory is actually exhausted.
It increases the managed heap size limit.
It pins objects in memory to prevent GC movement.

questions.text: How can async streams be used with LINQ-like operators?

options.text: Standard LINQ operators work directly on IAsyncEnumerable<T>.

The System.Linq.Async NuGet package (or similar) provides LINQ operators adapted for IAsyncEnumerable<T> (e.g., WhereAwait, SelectAwait, ToListAsync), enabling LINQ-style composition over async streams.
You must materialize async streams to a List<T> before using LINQ.
Async streams cannot be composed; each must be enumerated manually.

questions.text: What is a recursive pattern in C# 8+ and how does it combine other patterns?

options.text: A recursive pattern is a pattern that matches recursively nested objects of the same type.

A recursive pattern (positional or property) can nest other patterns within it: e.g., '{ Address: { City: { Length: > 3 } } }' nests property patterns; or a positional pattern can contain other positional/property patterns.
Recursive patterns only work with records.
Recursive patterns require the object to implement IRecursive.
